Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Consciousness Science

Pierre Janet

1859 – 1947

Dissociation, conduct, and the layered organization of conscious life.

Pierre Janet did not write a systematic “philosophy of mind” under that title. What he gives us is something more clinically grounded and, in some respects, philosophically richer: a psychology of mental synthesis, personality, conduct, belief, reality, and the subconscious, built from hypnosis, hysteria, psychasthenia, automatism, trauma, memory, religious ecstasy, and psychotherapy. Reconstructed as a philosophy of mind, Janet’s position is this:

Mind is not primarily a private inner theater, nor a merely physiological reflex-machine. Mind is organized conduct: a graded, embodied, social, temporally structured activity by which a living being synthesizes sensations, movements, memories, beliefs, affects, and purposes into a personality capable of acting in reality. Consciousness is the synthetic and integrative form of that activity. The “subconscious” is not sheer non-consciousness, but mental activity not gathered into personal consciousness. The soul, if we use that word carefully, is the fragile achievement of personal integration rather than a simple metaphysical substance directly given to introspection. Religious experience is therefore not an accidental side-topic: it is one of Janet’s most revealing test cases for belief, authority, love, embodiment, ecstasy, truth, and the limits of psychological explanation.

Janet’s own institutional profile already explains the hybridity of his thought. Britannica describes him as a French psychologist and neurologist who helped connect academic psychology with the clinical treatment of mental illness, studied automatic acts in his 1889 thesis, directed Charcot’s psychological laboratory at the Salpêtrière, and later held the Collège de France chair from 1902 to 1936. Henri Piéron’s French account emphasizes that Janet moved from an early problematic of consciousness and subconscience toward a concrete, objective “psychology of conduct,” while also noting his philosophical formation and his family connection to the spiritualist philosopher Paul Janet.

1. Method: Janet brackets metaphysical essence but does not abolish the soul-question

The first thing to notice is Janet’s restraint. In L’automatisme psychologique, he explicitly says that his work does not seek to penetrate either the ultimate nature of mind or the first cause of diseases; it seeks to analyze and understand the syndrome before speculating about causes. This is crucial. Janet is not saying that metaphysical questions are meaningless. He is saying that psychology, especially pathological psychology, must first describe and organize phenomena before it claims to know what mind “ultimately” is.

This methodological restraint is the key to handling “soul” language. Janet’s French terms are usually esprit, conscience, personnalité, conduite, croyance, tendance, and synthèse, rather than a doctrinal theology of l’âme. But he constantly studies phenomena traditionally assigned to the soul: will, freedom, responsibility, belief, religious faith, ecstasy, moral conduct, the sense of reality, the unity of the self, and the power of mind over body. He begins L’automatisme by saying that philosophers usually studied the highest forms of human activity—will, resolution, free will, responsibility, moral value—but that these higher forms are too complex to be understood first; he will instead study elementary, rudimentary human activity.

So Janet’s route to the soul is indirect. He does not start from the immortal soul or the Cartesian ego. He starts from the breakdown of synthesis. Where the person loses unity, memory, initiative, or reality-contact, the structure of the person becomes visible. His method is almost anatomical, but the “organ” dissected is not the brain alone; it is the organized life of consciousness and conduct.

That is why Janet is not a simple reductionist. A modern French reconstruction by Ortu, Craparo, and Van der Hart notes that Janet opposed treating mind and body as separate entities while also adopting natural-scientific methods for studying rudimentary human activity; they emphasize that he understood mental processes through conduct and visible action. In La médecine psychologique, he likewise warns that in attempts to modify human conduct we cannot radically separate the physical and the moral.

The result is not Cartesian dualism, not behaviorism, and not eliminative materialism. It is better described as clinical, embodied, action-oriented, non-reductive naturalism.

2. The basic ontology: mind as conduct, tendency, and synthesis

Janet’s mature psychology is a psychology of conduct. By 1928, he says quite explicitly that psychology is not merely the science of elementary reflexes; it concerns the conduct of human beings in general, including moral, religious, logical, philosophical, and even psychological conduct.

This is one of the deepest shifts in Janet’s thought. “Conduct” is broader than outward behavior. It includes bodily acts, speech, belief, memory, promise, hesitation, refusal, symbolic action, religious devotion, moral effort, and experimental verification. It is behavior plus meaning, direction, social form, and psychological organization.

The elements of mind are not inert sensations. They are tendencies toward action and organization. Janet’s world is made of acts, incipient acts, inhibited acts, substituted acts, and acts that have become automatic. This means that the mind is not a container of ideas but a hierarchy of tendencies. In De l’angoisse à l’extase, he describes levels of action: reflexive, perceptive, social, elementary intellectual acts, immediate wills and beliefs, reflective wills and beliefs, rational and experimental conduct, and progressive conduct. He then introduces the idea that people can move up or down this hierarchy depending on psychological tension.

Two principles organize this ontology.

The first is synthesis. Mental life becomes personal when many sensations, images, memories, and movements are gathered into one perception, one consciousness, one power of action. Weakness of synthesis produces dissociation, automatism, and fragmentation.

The second is psychological tension. Tension is not simply energy in a crude hydraulic sense. It is the level at which tendencies can be activated. High-tension conduct includes reflective belief, voluntary action, reality-contact, experimental verification, moral restraint, and freedom. Low-tension conduct falls back toward immediate belief, repetitive automatism, agitation, obsession, dreamlike reverie, or primitive assertion. Janet’s late works make tension the vertical dimension of mind, while the field of consciousness gives the horizontal dimension.

This yields a two-axis model of mind:

The horizontal axis is the width of integration: how many phenomena can be held together in one personal consciousness.

The vertical axis is the level of conduct: how high in the hierarchy of tendencies the person can operate.

A person can therefore fail horizontally, through dissociation and narrowing of the field, or vertically, through lowered tension and regression to lower forms of conduct. Hysteria is primarily the first type; psychasthenia is primarily the second.

3. Consciousness is not an epiphenomenon

Janet is explicit that consciousness cannot be dismissed as a useless accompaniment. In L’automatisme, he discusses the claim that coordination, intelligence, and sensibility could occur without consciousness, making consciousness a mere accessory or epiphenomenon. He rejects this as unintelligible: a “reasoning without consciousness,” he says, has no psychological meaning.

This does not mean that Janet thinks all consciousness is clear, reflective, verbal, or self-conscious. Quite the opposite. He distinguishes consciousness from personal self-awareness. There can be rudimentary consciousness, partial consciousness, or conscious images not attached to an “I.” He argues that in cataleptic and hysterical phenomena there is no good reason to exclude consciousness, because coordinated movements require sensations and images that guide them. Yet the subject’s ordinary consciousness can say, “I do not feel my arm moving,” which forces Janet to ask whether another consciousness can exist in the same mind.

This is perhaps Janet’s most radical philosophical move: consciousness is wider than the ego. There can be consciousness without personal ownership. There can be mental life that is conscious in some minimal or partial sense but not synthesized into the consciousness that says “I.”

From this follows a distinctively Janetian solution to a major problem in philosophy of mind. The question is not simply “Is a state conscious or unconscious?” The better question is: conscious for which system of synthesis? A state may be conscious as part of a partial system, yet unconscious for the personal self.

That is why Janet’s “subconscious” should not be equated too quickly with Freud’s unconscious. Janet’s subconscious is often a divided consciousness, not a dark reservoir of intrinsically unconscious contents.

4. The field of consciousness

Janet’s concept of the field of consciousness is one of his most important contributions. He borrows the analogy of the visual field and defines the field of consciousness as the greatest number of simple or relatively simple phenomena that can be present at once in one consciousness, while reserving attention for the “point of internal gaze.”

This is not merely attention in the narrow modern sense. The field of consciousness is the mind’s capacity for simultaneous integration. A wide field allows collateral sensations, memories, possible alternatives, social considerations, and future consequences to remain co-present. A narrow field forces consciousness into monoideism: one sensation, one image, one command, one obsession, one role, one fear.

This explains suggestibility. A person with a narrow field cannot hold enough countervailing tendencies together. The suggested idea occupies the available field and becomes action. Janet explicitly connects suggestibility with a restricted field and says this affects the will.

This is also the basis of Janet’s account of hysterical anesthesia and dissociation. In L’automatisme, he says that in certain patients sensitive phenomena are divided into two groups: one group is synthesized into personal perception and forms personal consciousness; the remaining phenomena are not synthesized into that perception.

The important point is that the excluded phenomena are not necessarily destroyed. They may continue to operate. They may guide movement, appear in automatic writing, produce bodily symptoms, or enter another personality-system.

So consciousness has three levels in Janet:

First, elementary consciousness: sensations and images with minimal organization.

Second, personal consciousness: sensations, images, and movements synthesized into an “I” and a power of action.

Third, reflective or high-level consciousness: belief, reality-contact, deliberation, social verification, moral and experimental conduct.

This layered model is much more subtle than a binary conscious/unconscious distinction.

5. The subconscious and dissociation: mental life outside the personal self

Janet’s model of dissociation is founded on a failure of synthesis. In the conclusion to his analyses of anesthesia and amnesia, he says his scientific hypothesis is not a claim about absolute reality, but a useful way to unify facts: psychological elements are as real and numerous as in normal persons, but because of a weakness of synthesis they cannot unite into one perception or one personal consciousness. The result is two or more simultaneous, incomplete groups of conscious phenomena.

This gives us a precise philosophical account of the subconscious:

The subconscious is not mere absence of consciousness.

It is not necessarily repressed desire in the Freudian sense.

It is not a second metaphysical soul.

It is a system of psychological phenomena not synthesized into the ordinary personal consciousness.

Modern Spanish-language Janet scholarship states the point clearly: dissociation, in Janet’s perspective, involves loss of synthesis and association of psychological functions, which move away from consciousness and are relegated to the subconscious; the same review emphasizes fixed ideas, narrowing of the field of consciousness, and weakness of mental synthesis as the relevant triad. The same article notes that Janet used “subconscious” because he considered psychological states to involve some degree of consciousness, even when below the threshold of normal consciousness.

This explains why dissociated functions can be intact. In Les Névroses, Janet says that despite dissociation, the separated function itself often remains nearly intact: memory can persist despite apparent amnesia, speech or walking can appear in dreams or somnambulism despite mutism or paralysis in the waking state. He then concludes that hysteria is above all a disease of personality, because it decomposes the ideas and functions whose union constitutes personal consciousness.

The philosophical implication is profound: the unity of mind is not guaranteed by the mere existence of mental contents. It is an achievement of synthesis. A sensation can occur, a memory can persist, an action can be prepared, and yet the person may not own it.

6. Automatism: not mechanism, but sedimented mind

Janet’s “automatism” is easily misunderstood. It does not mean that automatic acts are merely mechanical reflexes. In his early work, automatic activity is regular, lawful, and often predetermined, but still psychological. It can involve sensation, memory, coordination, and rudimentary consciousness.

Automatism is the afterlife of synthesis. Janet says that the automatic succession of images and acts is the continuation of a synthesis previously performed; when that old synthesis is reactivated, it tends to complete itself again. Present synthesis is therefore both the obstacle to automatism and the source of future automatisms.

Habit is not the enemy of mind. Habit is mind sedimented into reliable form. But when the field narrows or tension drops, old syntheses take over because the present self cannot reorganize them. Pathology occurs when automatism replaces adaptive present conduct.

This gives Janet a dynamic theory of agency. Agency is not the absence of automatism. Agency is the ability to synthesize automatisms into present, flexible, reality-oriented conduct. The healthy person is not non-automatic; the healthy person is able to subordinate automatic systems to current personal action.

7. Personality: the self as an achievement, not a primitive datum

Janet’s philosophy of self is anti-Cartesian in the following sense: the “I” is not the simple starting point of philosophy. It is a result of synthesis. Elementary conscious images can exist without the idea of the self; they do not form a personality until they are gathered into one thought, one perception, one system of action.

The person is therefore a functional unity, not an indivisible metaphysical atom given in introspection. Personality is the coordination of sensations, memories, movements, beliefs, social roles, bodily schema, and temporal orientation. In hysteria, this coordination fails. In psychasthenia, the person remains more unified but loses high-level reality-function, decision, confidence, and present action.

Janet’s account is especially important for philosophy because it separates three things that are often conflated:

There is consciousness: the occurrence of psychic phenomena.

There is personal consciousness: the synthesis of those phenomena into the “I.”

There is personality: the enduring organization of functions, memories, roles, tendencies, and powers of action.

Dissociation shows that consciousness does not automatically entail personality. Psychasthenia shows that personality does not automatically entail effective reality-contact. Religious ecstasy shows that intense belief and self-transcendence can heighten some forms of conviction while weakening ordinary reflective control.

A Janetian philosophy of the soul would therefore define the soul, not as a bare substance behind experience, but as the living unity of personal conduct. Its unity is real, but it is dynamic, vulnerable, and developmentally maintained.

8. Psychological tension and the hierarchy of mind

Janet’s later work introduces a vertical hierarchy of psychological functions. The person does not merely have more or fewer mental contents; the person operates at higher or lower levels. He describes oscillations of mental level: under various influences, the mind can ascend or descend the hierarchy of psychological tendencies.

This is where Janet’s concept of psychological tension becomes central. In Les Névroses, he says lowered psychological tension makes higher phenomena nearly impossible: the function of reality, voluntary action with feelings of freedom and personality, perception of reality, belief, certainty, and enjoyment of the present. Lower phenomena—disinterested perception, reasoning, reverie, motor and visceral agitation—can remain or even expand as substitutes for the suppressed higher functions.

This is not just a clinical theory. It is a philosophy of mental normativity. Higher mental life requires power. Freedom, belief, reality, certainty, and presence are not merely logical properties; they are achievements that require sufficient psychological tension.

Psychasthenia is the privileged example. Janet defines it as a form of mental depression marked by lowered psychological tension, diminished functions for acting on and perceiving reality, and substitution of inferior exaggerated operations such as doubt, agitation, anxiety, and obsessions.

This gives us a general Janetian law:

When high-level conduct fails, lower-level conduct does not simply disappear; it often proliferates.

A person unable to decide may ruminate endlessly. A person unable to act in reality may construct symbolic substitutes. A person unable to believe reflectively may oscillate between doubt and immediate credulity. A person unable to love socially may construct an idealized divine or imaginary relation. A person unable to complete action may experience incompleteness, unreality, depersonalization, or obsession.

9. The function of reality

Janet’s “function of the real” is one of his most philosophically fertile ideas. He defines it as the apprehension of reality by perception or action, and says it modifies other operations depending on whether it is added to them.

The real, for Janet, is not merely what is represented correctly. It is what can be acted upon, resisted, verified, modified, touched, socially negotiated, and integrated into present conduct. Reality-contact is therefore practical, perceptual, affective, and social.

This is why psychasthenia is not simply false belief. It is a diminished capacity to act on and perceive reality. A patient may reason, imagine, remember, and speak, but these operations no longer carry the feeling of present reality. The person can think about action but cannot complete it; can imagine the world but cannot inhabit it.

This is also why Janet’s account anticipates later phenomenological analyses of depersonalization and derealization. Reality is not just a proposition; it is a lived mode of contact. When reality-function drops, the world can remain intellectually recognized while losing its compelling presence.

10. Belief, truth, memory, and time as conduct

Janet’s late work on memory and time radicalizes his action theory. He says that belief is a way of linking words and actions; to believe something is to commit oneself to act in certain circumstances. He compares belief to promise, threat, engagement of honor, or pact.

This is a pragmatist theory of belief, but more clinical and social than a simple “belief is habit of action” formula. Belief is a conduct of assertion. It gives existence to something by binding the subject to a future pattern of action.

Memory is likewise not mere storage. Janet calls elementary memory an action deferred, an action waiting for circumstances in which it will be triggered. Memory becomes fully human when it is verbal, social, believable, and temporally organized.

His account of the present is especially important. The present, he says, is a narrative joined to action. There is no feeling of the present without narrative plus action; action brings affirmation of existence, and the present becomes identical with being or reality. Patients who say the present is unreal, Janet argues, no longer know how to construct the present because they do not feel action as completed.

This gives Janet a striking philosophy of time:

The past is not just what has vanished.

The future is not just what has not yet occurred.

The present is not just an instant.

Time is constructed through conduct: narrative, action, promise, memory, expectation, verification, and belief.

Hallucination then becomes a disorder of presentification. Janet says hallucination is not best understood simply as perception without an external object; it belongs to belief and faith. Extreme belief can transform a narrative into a present reality. Hallucination is an exaggerated and false presentification, making the distant present.

This is philosophically significant for theology as well. Janet’s analysis of faith does not simply say, “religious people hallucinate.” It says that belief has the power to confer presence. Religious presence, pathological hallucination, memory, and expectation all belong to the broader psychology of presentification. The discriminating question is not whether belief can make something present—it can—but whether the resulting presence is reflectively controlled, socially mediated, and integrated with action and reality.

11. Truth as reflective and experimental conduct

Janet’s account of truth is not correspondence theory in a purely abstract form. Truth is a higher conduct of belief. In De l’angoisse à l’extase, he distinguishes immediate belief from reflective belief. Immediate belief gives being a certain stability, but it is fragile and can change under influence. Reflective belief suspends precipitate affirmation, deliberates, confronts tendencies, and gives a more stable reality. He compresses this into a powerful formula: being is what is believed by immediate assent; the real is what is believed after reflection.

He further describes reflective belief as a slow affirmation after stopping tendencies, transforming them into verbal form, discussing and modifying propositions through deliberation, and adapting assertions to other people. Such beliefs become more social, more logical, more moral, more stable, and less dependent on momentary feeling.

By 1928, Janet ties this to experimental conduct. Experimental conduct is not only intellectual; it transforms feeling and action and is almost a moral conduct. It requires stopping belief, checking logic, touching or verifying, accepting the verdict of others and of nature, and being honest with oneself.

This is one of Janet’s most important contributions to philosophy of mind: truth is not just a property of propositions; it is a disciplined form of conduct. To be rational is to be able to delay assertion, submit belief to social and experiential checks, and reorganize action accordingly.

12. Religion and theology are not distractions in Janet; they are privileged evidence

Janet’s religious material must be treated seriously because it appears at every decisive level of his theory: belief, suggestion, miracle, ecstasy, embodiment, authority, love, truth, and the relation between psychological and metaphysical interpretation.

In La médecine psychologique, Janet begins the history of psychotherapy with miraculous or magical cures. He does not treat them as arbitrary nonsense. He argues that even miracles, considered as events with repeated conditions, are not as arbitrary as one might think; the task is to investigate their determinism. He points to pilgrimage, waiting, marvelous narratives, religious exaltation, public séances, and emotion as psychological causes of major disturbance, and says that the domain of psychology is where one must seek the determinism of such effects.

At the same time, he criticizes vague appeals to “the power of mind.” He says that some psychotherapies speak of faith, belief, truth, idealism, morality, imagination, or expectant attention, but often use these terms imprecisely; the psychological phenomena involved are complex and different from one another.

This gives us Janet’s basic stance toward religion: neither credulous acceptance nor crude dismissal. Religious phenomena are real psychological phenomena. They may produce bodily and mental effects. But they must be analyzed into specific operations: suggestion, belief, expectation, social authority, emotion, exhaustion, automatism, and presentification.

Spiritism and “foreign spirits”

Janet’s work on automatism includes phenomena that religious or popular cultures interpret as spirits. He discusses divining rods, pendulums, thought-reading, and meaningful involuntary movements. When such movements express an idea or warning, people naturally interpret them as an intelligence foreign to humanity, good or bad, to be feared or implored. Janet’s analysis is that these are subconscious movements and dissociated intelligences, not necessarily alien spirits. Italian reception of L’automatismo psicologico also emphasizes that Janet studied hysteria, hypnosis, suggestion, spiritism, trauma, and the relation between personality and consciousness in constructing a scientific psychology.

Here again, the theological importance is not eliminable. Janet is showing how the experience of an “other will” can arise from within divided agency. But that does not logically prove that every religious claim to otherness is false. It shows that the form of otherness can be psychologically generated. Theology, if careful, must therefore distinguish between the psychological vehicle of alterity and the metaphysical status of the alterity encountered.

Ecstasy, inspiration, and mystical experience

Janet’s De l’angoisse à l’extase is indispensable for the theological dimension. He defines complete ecstasy as involving religious ideas and feelings: a divine life, a life in God, intense conviction, automatisms, inspiration, revelations, prophecies, assertions of presence, and happy feelings of purity, admiration, and conviction.

He then interprets many mystical states as regressions toward what he calls assertive thought: immediate affirmation determined by feeling, without the nuances of reflective reality. He applies this to Madeleine’s ecstasies, saying she mixes times, places, and identities, and believes in participations. The language here is historically dated and entangled with early twentieth-century evolutionary assumptions about “primitive” thought; it must be used critically rather than adopted wholesale. But the structural point remains important: ecstatic certainty can be immediate, affect-driven, and weakly controlled by reflective distinctions.

Janet also recognizes that religious and philosophical interpreters object to his reduction. He explicitly notes the view that mystical states might be “windows” onto a larger and more complete world, and he discusses philosophers who see the annihilation of normal thought as liberation from ordinary perception and logic, allowing a deeper contact with reality. That passage matters. Janet does not simply ignore the theological counterclaim. He registers it, then refuses to make it his scientific conclusion.

This is exactly where a rigorous theological reading should begin. Janet does not prove that ecstasy is false. He argues that, as a psychologist, he can describe the form of belief, the level of conduct, the bodily conditions, and the social consequences. The metaphysical question remains bracketed.

Stigmata: neither fraud nor pure miracle

Janet’s discussion of stigmata is similarly subtle. In Madeleine’s case, he observes physiological susceptibility of the skin, circulation, swelling, pressure, posture, and moral dispositions. He does not reduce the matter to lying or simulation. He says both physiological and moral conditions play a role.

The theological implication is important: embodied religious signs need not be forced into a binary of “miracle or fraud.” Janet makes room for psychophysiological mediation. A believer could interpret such mediation as the natural vehicle of grace; a skeptic could interpret it as psychopathology; a psychologist can describe the conditions without deciding the theology.

God as lover, father, teacher, sovereign, and director

Janet’s analysis of Madeleine’s relation to God is one of the most philosophically important parts of his religious psychology. He notes that Madeleine’s God is not merely an abstract theological object. God functions as sovereign, child, professor, father, and lover; these roles resemble human relations, but divine invisibility and omnipotence facilitate and perfect the union without changing its psychological nature.

He then makes a decisive methodological clarification. When he says that Madeleine’s God “is not true” in a certain sense, he immediately specifies that he is not speaking of religious or metaphysical truth, but only psychological truth. Psychological truth, for Janet, belongs to reflective belief controlled by social laws, reason, and experience.

This is a crucial distinction. Janet is not writing theology. He is saying that Madeleine’s private divine relation escapes the controls that ordinarily stabilize belief. God’s invisibility, omnipotence, and inscrutability allow the belief to evade ordinary verification, social correction, and rational challenge. In periods of conviction, Madeleine can answer requests for proof by saying that Janet’s eyes do not know how to see invisible things; Janet comments that such beliefs are well protected from examination.

Janet also sees a psychodynamic advantage in the divine director. Human directors must guess the patient’s desire; Madeleine’s God knows the desire perfectly because the same person feels the desire and makes God speak. Divine authority then adds an excitant to the will, while the command remains adapted to the desire.

This is psychologically severe, but not trivial. It suggests that “God” can function as an externalized executive synthesis: a source of decision, authorization, simplification, and affective security when the person cannot decide or act by herself. In a pathological case, that can short-circuit reality-testing. In a non-pathological theological context, something analogous may occur in disciplined forms of prayer, obedience, spiritual direction, and vocation—but with communal, doctrinal, ethical, and practical checks.

A theological correction to Janet: Madeleine may not be merely “delusional material”

Modern French commentary by Pierre-Henri Castel warns against flattening Madeleine’s experience into Janet’s categories. Castel argues that Janet did not ignore the politico-religious and social context, but that merely calling her mysticism a code for suffering risks failing to hear her voice. Castel also emphasizes the transferential scene: Madeleine calls Janet “mon Père,” and the clinical relation itself becomes part of the authentication and transmission of her mystical writings.

Most importantly, Castel reverses Janet’s conclusion. Rather than saying that Madeleine’s obsession with direction simply becomes a delusion of union with God, Castel argues that her union with the divine Other governs her relation to Janet: she obeys Janet, but God limits Janet’s arbitrary hypnotic power, allowing her to remain free in obedience.

This is a serious theological and philosophical challenge to Janet. Janet’s psychology is powerful, but it may under-read the internal logic of religious obedience, spiritual authorship, and divine alterity. A comprehensive Janetian philosophy of mind must therefore include both Janet’s analysis and a critique of his reductive tendencies.

13. Janet’s relation to Freud and psychoanalysis

Janet’s philosophy of mind is often overshadowed by Freud, but the relationship is more complex than simple rivalry. A Collège de France account notes that Freud and Janet cited each other, not always hostilely; Freud acknowledged Janet as the first to penetrate more deeply into the psychical processes of hysteria, and Freud later invoked Janet’s function of reality. The same account notes that Janet considered psychoanalysis in relation to his own earlier psychological analysis and traumatic-memory work, questioning psychoanalysis’s novelty.

Philosophically, the difference is this:

Freud’s mature model emphasizes repression, conflict, wish, sexuality, symbolic compromise, and the dynamic unconscious.

Janet emphasizes weakness of synthesis, narrowing of the field, dissociation, fixed ideas, psychological tension, and the loss of reality-function.

In Freud, unconscious contents are often unacceptable wishes excluded by conflict. In Janet, subconscious systems are often unsynthesized functions or fixed ideas continuing outside personal consciousness because the integrative power of the person has failed.

This makes Janet especially relevant for trauma theory, dissociation, functional neurological symptoms, and disorders of agency. Spanish-language reception explicitly connects Janet’s concepts to trauma and PTSD, while Italian reception highlights his importance for dissociation, trauma, and the relation between personality and consciousness.

14. The “soul” reconstructed from Janet

A Janetian philosophy of the soul would not begin with the question, “What immaterial substance thinks?” It would begin with these questions:

What allows sensations, memories, movements, beliefs, and affects to belong to one person?

What allows a person to act in the present rather than merely repeat the past?

What gives reality its force?

What makes belief stable, social, and true rather than immediate, private, and hallucinatory?

What gives freedom its felt and practical actuality?

What happens when part of the person continues to act outside the personal self?

From Janet’s work, the soul can be reconstructed as the integrative power of a living person to synthesize conduct at a high level of tension in relation to reality, others, time, and value.

This is not a denial of theological soul-language. It is a psychological translation of the soul’s functional unity. The soul, in Janet’s clinical sense, is not merely “inside.” It is enacted through bodily posture, memory, speech, promises, prayer, work, belief, love, obedience, attention, experimental verification, and social reality.

The soul is therefore:

Embodied, because Janet refuses a radical separation of physical and moral life.

Synthetic, because personality depends on the unification of functions.

Graded, because conduct occurs at higher or lower levels of psychological tension.

Temporal, because memory and the present are constructed through narrative and action.

Social, because reflective belief, truth, and personality depend on relation to others.

Vulnerable, because dissociation shows that mental life can fragment.

Religious-capable, because belief, presence, authority, love, and ecstasy can take divine forms.

Not self-transparent, because much mental life can continue outside the “I.”

This is a much more dynamic soul than the introspective ego of classical rationalism.

15. A compact Janetian philosophy of mind: twelve theses

A rigorous extraction of Janet’s philosophy can be formulated as twelve theses.

First, psychology must bracket metaphysical ultimates without denying their importance. Janet refuses to begin with the essence of mind or first causes; he begins with observable organization and breakdown.

Second, mind is conduct. Psychology concerns human actions at every level, including moral, religious, logical, and philosophical action.

Third, consciousness is functionally indispensable. Janet rejects the idea that consciousness is a useless epiphenomenon attached to intelligent action.

Fourth, consciousness is broader than personal consciousness. There can be conscious images and sensations without the idea of the self.

Fifth, personal consciousness is synthesis. The “I” arises when sensations, images, memories, and movements are united into one perception, one power, one personality.

Sixth, the subconscious is dissociated consciousness, not simple non-being. Subconscious phenomena can be real, organized, and effective while not belonging to ordinary personal consciousness.

Seventh, automatism is the continuation of past synthesis. Habit and suggestion are not merely mechanical; they are older organizations reactivating when present synthesis is weak.

Eighth, mental pathology reveals normal structure. Hysteria exposes failures of personal synthesis; psychasthenia exposes lowered tension and loss of reality-function.

Ninth, reality is apprehended by action and perception. The real is not merely represented; it is acted on, resisted, verified, and inhabited.

Tenth, belief is conduct. To believe is to bind speech to possible action, like a promise or pact.

Eleventh, truth requires reflective and experimental discipline. Mature belief is slowed, socialized, checked by reason, and verified by experience.

Twelfth, religious experience is psychologically central. It reveals how belief confers presence, how authority can organize will, how the body can express meaning, and how the soul seeks unity through love, obedience, and transcendence.

16. Critical evaluation

Janet’s system has major strengths. It avoids reducing consciousness either to introspective privacy or to physiological reflex. It explains dissociation without denying the reality of mental activity outside the ego. It connects attention, agency, memory, temporality, belief, and social verification in a single theory. It anticipates later action-based, embodied, trauma-oriented, and dissociation-oriented models of mind. Contemporary French, Spanish, and Italian receptions all continue to treat Janet as crucial for trauma, dissociation, functional symptoms, and the relation between consciousness and personality.

But there are also limits.

First, Janet’s hierarchy of mental levels sometimes uses historically dated evolutionary and colonial categories. His references to “primitive” or “prelogical” thought should not be imported uncritically into contemporary philosophy or theology. The structural distinction between immediate and reflective belief remains useful; the anthropological ranking needs revision.

Second, his concepts of force and tension are clinically powerful but physiologically underdetermined. Janet himself admits that the physiological mechanism remains unknown and that psychology can determine only the psychological aspect with precision.

Third, Janet does not fully solve the metaphysical problem of consciousness. He rejects epiphenomenalism and studies conscious organization, but he does not give a final account of why consciousness exists. This is not a failure by his own standards; it follows from his methodological bracketing.

Fourth, his religious analyses are sometimes reductive. He often explains mystical union through love, authority, economy of effort, regression, and immediate belief. Those analyses are illuminating, but they do not settle the theological truth of mystical experience. Castel’s reading of Madeleine shows that Janet’s own material can support a more theologically sensitive interpretation than Janet himself always gives.

Conclusion

Pierre Janet’s philosophy of mind is best understood as a philosophy of integration. Mind is the organized conduct of a living being. Consciousness is not a passive light but a synthetic function that gathers phenomena into personal action. The self is not a primitive certainty but an achievement. The subconscious is not mere darkness but dissociated mental organization. Reality is not just represented; it is enacted through perception, action, belief, and verification. Truth is not merely a proposition’s abstract correctness; it is a disciplined conduct of belief. Memory and time are not containers but narrative-action structures. Religion is not a side-issue but one of the most intense domains in which belief, presence, authority, love, and bodily meaning become visible.

A Janetian “soul” is therefore the person’s dynamic power of synthesis: the capacity to hold together body, memory, action, belief, affect, social relation, and reality in a unified life. Its disorders are dissociation, automatism, lowered tension, loss of reality, uncontrolled belief, and false presentification. Its healing is not merely insight but reintegration: widening the field of consciousness, raising psychological tension, restoring action in the present, disciplining belief, and reconnecting the person to reality and others.

Theologically, Janet should be neither dismissed as a reductive psychiatrist nor treated as a theologian in disguise. He is a psychologist whose work forces theology to become more precise. He shows how religious experience can organize or disorganize the soul, how divine authority can support or evade agency, how faith can produce presence, how the body can bear belief, and how mystical certainty can be both psychologically intelligible and metaphysically undecided. His deepest contribution is that he makes the soul clinically visible without pretending to exhaust its mystery.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
